name: Poland
description: The North European Plain between the Oder and Bug rivers — the Vistula basin. Homeland of the West Slavic Piast dynasty; one of the largest Medieval European states.

periods.note: West Slavic settlement — Germanic Goths and other peoples occupied the Vistula region before migrating south and west during the Migration Period. West Slavic peoples (ancestors of Poles, Czechs, Slovaks) moved in and settled as farmers. By 800 CE the Polans tribe was dominant around Gniezno — proto-Polish state forming.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — West Slavic tribal confederations consolidating

periods.note: Early Piast Poland — Mieszko I's baptism in 966 is the founding date of the Polish state. His son Bolesław I the Brave (r. 992–1025) was crowned first King of Poland by the Pope (1025). Poland expanded east and west; temporarily held Bohemia and Moravia. Divided among Piast princes after Bolesław III's death (1138) — fragmentation began.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — West Slavic Polish identity consolidating around Catholicism

periods.note: Mongol invasion — Batu Khan's forces devastated Poland (1241), sacking Kraków and defeating the Polish-German force at Legnica. The Mongols withdrew without permanently occupying Poland, but the devastation was severe. Teutonic Knights began colonising Polish Baltic lands in this era.

Reunified Piast → Jagiellonian Poland — Władysław I reunited Poland 1320. Casimir III the Great (1333–1370) rebuilt the kingdom and famously welcomed Jewish refugees from Western Europe, making Poland the centre of European Jewry. Jagiellonian dynasty from 1386 (through Jadwiga's marriage to Jogaila of Lithuania) — the personal union with Lithuania created the largest state in 15th-century Europe. Defeat of the Teutonic Knights at Grunwald (1410).

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Polish Catholic identity; Old Polish language
significant and growing — Poland became the most important centre of Jewish life in the world (Ashkenazi Judaism)
urban merchant and artisan communities — Magdeburg rights cities

periods.note: Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (Rzeczpospolita) — one of the largest states in Europe; elected monarchy; noble democracy (szlachta); the liberum veto gave any noble the power to dissolve parliament. Fought wars against Russia, Sweden, the Ottomans. Jan Sobieski saved Vienna from the Ottomans (1683). The liberum veto became a tool for foreign powers (Russia, Prussia) to paralyse the state. Partitioned three times (1772, 1793, 1795) and disappeared from the map.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant in Poland proper; political class
largest Jewish community in the world — towns of the eastern borderlands often Jewish-majority
Ukrainian and Belarusian populations in the vast eastern territories

periods.note: Partitioned Poland — divided between Russia (largest share), Prussia, and Austria; Polish state ceased to exist for 123 years. Polish identity survived through language, Catholicism, and repeated failed uprisings (1794, 1830, 1863). Napoleon briefly created the Duchy of Warsaw (1807–1815). Polish culture flourished in exile: Chopin, Mickiewicz, Słowacki.

ethnic_composition.presence: preserved majority population under foreign rule
largest Jewish population in the world concentrated in Russian-ruled Congress Poland

periods.note: Second Republic (1918–1939) — Poland reborn after WWI. Invaded simultaneously by Nazi Germany and Soviet Union in September 1939; Warsaw Uprising 1944 crushed by the Germans. Holocaust: 3 million Polish Jews (90%) murdered; 3 million Polish Catholics also killed — Poland lost 22% of its population, the highest proportional loss of any nation in WWII. Communist People's Republic 1944–1989; Solidarity movement; transition to democracy 1989.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — post-WWII Poland made ethnically homogeneous through genocide, expulsion, and border shifts
